summary: Percent change is always measured relative to the original (starting) value, and successive percent changes don't simply add together.
explanation: Percent change is always computed relative to the ORIGINAL value, not the new one — a common trap is dividing by the wrong number. Applying a percent increase of \(r\) multiplies the original by \((1+r)\); a decrease multiplies by \((1-r)\). A critical GRE trap: successive percent changes do not add. A 20% increase followed by a 20% decrease does NOT return to the original value — it multiplies by \(1.2 \times 0.8 = 0.96\), a net 4% decrease, because the second change applies to a different (larger or smaller) base than the first. To reverse a percent change and find the original value, divide by \((1\pm r)\) rather than just applying the opposite percent to the new value.
